success_criteria.description: All five districts are in use and every precinct is assigned.
All five districts have roughly equal population (within 5%).
All districts must be reasonably compact — no long tentacles or thin strips (compactness ≥ 50%).
Even a geometrically neutral map has an efficiency gap — see how close to zero you can get (≤ 15%).

character.name: You
character.motivation: The legislature has deadlocked. A court has ordered an independent commission to draw a new map using only neutral criteria — equal population, geographic compactness, and contiguity. No partisan data allowed. Draw the fairest map you can.

intro_slides.heading: The Promise of Neutral Rules
intro_slides.body:
Reformers have long argued that if we just take politics out of redistricting — use an independent commission, apply mechanical criteria, ignore partisan data — the maps will be fair.

Meridian County is your test case. Draw a map using only geometric rules: compact districts, equal population, no disconnected pieces. No partisan data. Just shapes.

intro_slides.heading: The Geography Problem
intro_slides.body:
Here's what nobody tells you: voters aren't distributed randomly. In Meridian County — as in most American counties — different communities cluster together. Urban neighborhoods vote differently from suburbs, which vote differently from exurbs.

A perfectly geometric map doesn't erase that. It just captures it differently.

intro_slides.heading: What the Map Will Show You
intro_slides.body:
When you're done, submit your map and look at the results. You drew it without looking at party data. But the outcome won't be perfectly proportional.

This is called the geography problem. It's not a bug in your map — it's a feature of where people live. Neutral rules don't produce neutral outcomes. They produce different outcomes.

narrative.objective: Draw a compact, population-balanced map using only geometric criteria. No partisan data required — or allowed.
